verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Petilianus said: "This being the case, brethren, what perversity must it be, that he who is guilty through his own sins should make another free from guilt, when the Lord Jesus Christ says, ‘Every good tree bringeth forth good fruit, but a corrupt tree bringeth forth evil fruit: do men gather grapes of thorns?’

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And again: ‘A good man, out of the good treasure of the heart, bringeth forth good things: and an evil man, out of the evil treasure, bringeth forth evil things.’"

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Augustin answered: No man, even though he be not guilty through his own sins, can make his neighbor free from sin, because he is not God.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Otherwise, if we were to expect that out of the innocence of the baptizer should be produced the innocence of the baptized, then each will be the more innocent in proportion as he may have found a more innocent person by whom to be baptized; and will himself be the less innocent in proportion as he by whom he is baptized is less innocent.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: And if the man who baptizes happens to entertain hatred against another man, this will also be imputed to him who is baptized.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Why, therefore, does the wretched man hasten to be baptized,—that his own sins may be forgiven him, or that those of others may be reckoned against him?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: Is he like a merchant ship, to discharge one burden, and to take on him another?

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But by the good tree and its good fruit, and the corrupt tree and its evil fruit, we are wont to understand men and their works, as is consequently shown in those other words which you also quoted: "A good man, out of the good treasure of his heart, bringeth forth good things: and an evil man, out of the evil treasure, bringeth forth evil things."

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: But when a man preaches the word of God, or administers the sacraments of God, he does not, if he is a bad man, preach or minister out of his own treasure; but he will be counted among those of whom it is said, "Whatsoever they bid you observe, that observe and do; but do not ye after their works:" for they bid you observe what is God’s, but their works are their own.

verses.book: Answer to the Letters of Petilian, the Donatist.
verses.text: For if it is as you say, that is, if the fruit of those who baptize consist in the baptized persons themselves, you declare a great woe against Africa, if a young Optatus has sprung up for every one that Optatus baptized.
